FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the ABCD Study Data Release 3.0, 11,878 participants at baseline, 21 U.S. sites, ages 9-10 years at baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using bivariate multiple indicator latent change score models relating lifestyle risk factors to brain structure at baseline and 2-year follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Health Psychology (2023); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in the paper. Screen time is one indicator loading onto a single latent 'lifestyle risk domain' together with sleep, physical activity, and BMI; all results report the composite domain, not screen time alone. Step 2: The composite includes non-phone/non-SM ingredients (sleep duration, physical activity, BMI) without a separate phone/SM breakout — fails Step 2. Even if screen time were isolated, the Youth Screen Time Survey (Bagot et al., 2018) measures total recreational screen time (weekday + weekend) as a single composite without isolating phone/SM modalities. No per-modality coefficient is reported anywhere in main tables, supplementary, or sensitivity analyses.

notes: Screen time is only one of four lifestyle risk indicators folded into a single latent composite domain. No phone/SM-specific estimate is reported anywhere. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data drawn from ABCD Study Data Release 4.0, 11,878 participants, 21 U.S. sites, ages 9-12 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: Group Factor Analysis (GFA) of screen media activity and resting-state fMRI, GAMMs predicting mental health and cognitive outcomes, and longitudinal network analyses of functional connectome change.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Cortex (2023); registered report; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Section 3.7 reports an exploratory analysis using only time spent on social media platforms ("Visit social networking sites like Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, etc.") as a standalone predictor of cognitive and wellbeing outcomes, with beta coefficients ranging from .013 to .026 (reported in OSM-4). Social media is a named-platform SM variable; the outcomes (picture vocabulary, internalizing/externalizing symptoms, cognitive measures from Table 3) are individual-trait-level. This constitutes a phone/SM-specific estimate paired with individual-trait outcomes, satisfying Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Beta coefficients for social media predicting cognitive and wellbeing outcomes are reported (range .013 to .026, p-values reported); standardized coefficients also shown in Figs. 7 and 8 for GFA factors.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported: GFA factor loadings, standardized GAMM coefficients with 95% CIs (e.g., SMA_GFA1 beta = .052, p < .001 for picture vocabulary; Table 2 accounted covariance values; Table 4 linear regression betas for network metrics), and social-media-specific betas.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is not exclusively longitudinal SM-as-DV. The main confirmatory analyses are cross-sectional with screen media activity as IV predicting brain connectivity and functional outcomes. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Primary analyses use composite SMA profiles as predictors of brain/cognitive/wellbeing outcomes (SM as IV). The exploratory social-media-specific analysis (section 3.7) additionally provides a named-platform SM estimate paired with individual-trait outcomes. INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from ABCD Study (4.0 release), 5797 participants aged 10-14 years, 21 racially and ethnically diverse U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear regression and modified Poisson regression examining associations of screen time categories and step count categories with BMI percentile and overweight/obesity.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open (2023); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found. Screen time is operationalized as a composite total from the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire: 'television shows and movies, videos, video games (single player and multiplayer), texting, social media, and video chat' summed into total recreational screen time hours per day. No separate coefficient for any phone/SM-specific modality (texting, social media, video chat) is reported anywhere in main tables, supplementary, or sensitivity analyses. Step 2: The composite includes non-phone/non-SM ingredients (television/movies, video games) without a separate breakout. Per the methods: 'The time across all types of media was summed to form a composite amount of time spent using screens per weekday and weekend.' Fails Step 2.

notes: Screen time composite includes TV/movies and video games bundled with texting/social media/video chat without any per-modality breakout. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.
